Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends since I work during the week, but it rarely went according to plan. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often spend time practicing things rather than just reading, which meant my actual study hours felt productive even if they weren't that long. With 12 courses to juggle, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. I'd have these bursts of activity right before deadlines, then quieter periods where I barely touched anything. Life stuff always seemed to get in the way—work schedules, family obligations. I tried to be consistent but I'm not going to lie, consistency isn't my strong suit.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline coming up or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed. Fear of falling behind was honestly a big motivator—I'd get this scared feeling that I was missing something important and that would push me to check in.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. It never became automatic for me like checking my phone or email. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I got into a groove, but then something would throw me off and I'd fall out of the habit. I think because I learn better by doing physical things rather than clicking through screens, the LMS always felt like a chore.
Q5: The video content was actually helpful when I used it—I'd watch them and then try to apply what I learned practically. Discussion boards were less useful to me personally, I only posted once the whole course. What really helped was when peers would explain things in simpler terms during our study group chats, that peer support made concepts click better than the formal materials.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums after my one required post. They felt performative and I didn't see how they helped me actually learn the material. Some of the supplementary readings I'd open and then close immediately—with limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate practical application or if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. I need to do things with my hands, engage physically with concepts somehow. If something was just theoretical reading with no clear purpose, I'd struggle to justify spending my limited study hours on it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With 12 courses and limited time and resources, I had to be ruthless about what I engaged with. Required stuff got done because it had to, but optional things got pushed aside even when I knew they might help. There's a guilt that comes with that, but survival mode doesn't leave room for extras.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download PDFs and videos when I had good internet access, then study them offline at home where my connection is unreliable. I also had a study group through the peer support program where we'd meet in person and work through problems together. A lot of my real learning happened with printed notes and physical practice, not clicking through the LMS.
Q10: My peer study sessions were huge for my learning but left zero trace online. We'd meet at someone's place or a community center and work through problems on paper. I'd also practice skills physically—like if it was something hands-on, I'd actually do it rather than just read about it. The LMS would show me as inactive during some of my most productive learning periods.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video lecture once, maybe watch the first few minutes online, then finish it offline over several days. Same with readings—I'd access them once to download, then print them out if I could afford to, or read them on my phone offline. My one-time access definitely didn't reflect how much I actually engaged with those materials.
Q12: There were definitely low points, usually when multiple deadlines collided or when I was dealing with money stress. Being scared about whether I could keep up with everything made it hard to focus. Sometimes the anxiety would paralyze me and I'd avoid the LMS entirely because logging in meant confronting how behind I felt.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about bills, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion about course material would make me avoid it too—if I didn't understand something, I'd put off engaging with it rather than feeling stupid. Competing responsibilities always won over coursework when push came to shove.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was elsewhere, worrying about other things. I'd have a video playing but not really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions just to feel like I was doing something, even though I wasn't really learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt pointless to me. Also avoided starting big assignments until the last minute because the fear of not doing them well enough kept me from starting at all. It's this weird cycle where being scared of failing makes you procrastinate, which makes failure more likely. I know it's not logical but that's how it went.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd have this surge of activity in the days before something was due, then go quiet again. My LMS logins probably looked like a heartbeat monitor—spikes and flatlines. Without deadlines, I honestly wouldn't have engaged much at all. They were stressful but necessary.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did motivate me to try harder on the next thing, especially if it was constructive. But feedback often came late, so by then I'd already moved on mentally. The peer support helped more—getting immediate reactions from study partners kept me more engaged than waiting for official feedback.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible which was both good and bad. Good because I could work around my schedule, bad because without strict structure I'd drift. Having 12 courses meant I couldn't give deep attention to any single one. The ones with clearer weekly expectations were easier to keep up with.
Q19: Courses that had hands-on components or practical applications were way easier for me to engage with—that's just how I learn. The ones that were all reading and theory felt like swimming upstream. Also, when materials were easy to download for offline use, that helped a lot given my internet situation.
Q20: I think sometimes people are searching for something, trying to understand, clicking around without finding what they need. Or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just digital pacing, you know?
Q21: That's kind of me, honestly. My LMS activity was low but I passed. A lot of my learning happened offline or with peers, in ways the system couldn't track. I'd access something once, study it thoroughly offline, and perform okay on assessments. The LMS only sees part of the picture.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active and what you click on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss offline study, group learning, the mental work of processing information. They definitely can't capture whether someone was genuinely engaged or just going through the motions while stressed and scared.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, when you want to understand rather than just pass. For me, engagement happens when I can physically do something with what I'm learning, when it connects to real life. It's not about how many times I log in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to get through this course—those conversations and study sessions were lifelines. It wouldn't show my fear and anxiety, or how I'd sometimes avoid logging in because I was scared of what I'd find. It also can't capture that despite low engagement numbers, I was genuinely trying my best with limited resources and time.
